# 1. Introduction

Climate change is reshaping the conditions under which the world's food is produced. The Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change has established that the accumulation of greenhouse gases is driving significant changes in Earth's climate [@ipcc2007; @ipcc2013], and these changes threaten agriculture through rising temperatures [@lobell2011], shifting precipitation patterns [@yuan2024], more frequent extreme weather [@lesk2016], and dwindling water supplies [@liu2022]. For the major feed grains, empirical work has consistently linked these shifts to marked yield declines [@schlenker2009; @tack2015; @zhang2017; @shew2020; @malikov2020]. Far less is known about how the same forces affect pasture and forage, the feed base on which grazing livestock depend, although the limited evidence available points to adverse effects [@gauly2013].

This gap matters because the United States livestock industry, among the largest in the world, depends heavily on a stable supply of feed grains and forage crops [@simmons1966; @marsh2007], making domestic forage a matter of national rather than merely regional consequence. Warming presses on this system from several directions at once. Longer and more severe droughts can force ranchers toward alternative feed sources [@kemper2013], a pressure compounded by competition between livestock and humans for major grain crops [@mottet2017], with implications for domestic food availability and exports through both direct grain consumption and its indirect consumption as livestock feed. Warming may also redraw the geography of forage production, raising the cost of sourcing and transporting feed for producers who use it to supplement or replace grazing [@kemper2013]. These risks are not hypothetical: the 2012 nationwide drought degraded more than 70% of U.S. pasturelands and cut cow-calf returns by more than \$100 per cow [@kemper2013]. Warming also acts on the animals themselves, changing water use [@ahlberg2018], production [@chen2024; @summer2019], reproduction [@wolfenson2019], and the mix of available forage [@xie2022]: daily water consumption in beef cattle rises by about 0.65 kg for each degree Celsius above roughly 25°C, heat stress lowers feed intake and with it milk and beef output, and dairy conception rates fall by an additional 5% when summer temperatures run 1.5°C above average [@wolfenson2019]. As conditions warm, the forage base itself tends to shift from cool-season (C3) toward warm-season (C4) species better suited to hotter, drier conditions [@xie2022].

Alfalfa is the natural focus for this problem. It ranks as the fourth-largest U.S. crop by acreage [@usda_quickstats] and is prized for its high protein content and nitrogen-fixing ability, which reduces reliance on synthetic fertilizers.^[An acre of alfalfa, on average, will fix about 200 kg of nitrogen per year.] In 2019 the United States harvested more than 54 million tons of alfalfa worth roughly \$8.4 billion, part of a hay sector spanning about 128 million acres and valued near \$17 billion [@usda_quickstats]. Production is concentrated in the northwestern United States, especially California, Idaho, Nevada, Oregon, Wyoming, and Washington (Figure 1), a geographic concentration that itself signals exposure to climatic change. Yet despite alfalfa's standing as a primary protein source for the nation's cattle, the tools used to quantify climate impacts on field crops have not been brought to bear on it. The endogenous piecewise degree-day framework that underpins much of the grain-yield literature was developed for and applied to grain crops [@tack2015; @schlenker2009], and climate-focused yield analysis for forage remains sparse relative to field crops [@zhang2017; @shew2020; @malikov2020]. County-level estimates of the heat thresholds and yield penalties specific to U.S. alfalfa are absent from the published literature, and while related work documents climate risks to dairy systems, it is set in Central Europe rather than the United States [@gauly2013]. As a result, the forage and livestock-feed supply chain, and how warming might disrupt it, has been left largely unexamined.

This study addresses that gap with two linked questions: whether warming has a measurable effect on alfalfa hay yields, and whether projected warming will shift the crop's spatial distribution. To answer them, we assemble a county-level panel spanning 1951 to 2022 and apply established models of the temperature-yield relationship [@schlenker2009; @tack2015; @shew2020] to estimate the temperature thresholds above which alfalfa yields begin to decline and the rate at which they fall as exposure to harmful heat rises. We then use these estimates to simulate how uniform warming scenarios would redistribute alfalfa production across the country.

Three findings stand out. First, the temperature-yield relationship is strongly nonlinear: exposure to beneficial heat, between `r fmt_degC(objs$knots$lower)` and `r fmt_degC(objs$knots$upper)`, is positively associated with alfalfa yields, whereas exposure above `r fmt_degC(objs$knots$upper)` is detrimental. Second, uniform warming produces a progression of yield losses that deepens from mild to severe scenarios across 0.5°C to 3°C of warming. Third, the impacts are geographically uneven and track agro-climatic zones. Cooler counties in the Northeast, including Connecticut, Rhode Island, and Massachusetts, tend to gain as warming eases heat stress, whereas the South and parts of the Midwest and West decline, with the sharpest losses in states such as Texas, Louisiana, Oklahoma, Kansas, and Arizona. Even a 0.5°C increase lifts yields in some northern states while lowering them across the South, where Florida falls by `r fmt_abs_pct(objs$impact_state$FL$s05, 2)`; under 3°C of warming the losses intensify to `r fmt_abs_pct(objs$impact_state$FL$s30, 2)` in Florida and `r fmt_abs_pct(objs$impact_state$TX$s30, 2)` in Texas, and most northern gains diminish or reverse beyond about 2°C. Harmful-heat sensitivity is steepest in the hot, humid South and close to flat in the heavily irrigated Southwest, though we caution that this apparent resilience is conditional on irrigation.

The consequences do not stop at the field. Because county cattle inventory depends on local forage, a +1°C warming lowers alfalfa availability by `r fmt_abs_pct(objs$impact_avail$s10)` and cattle inventory by `r fmt_abs_pct(objs$impact_cattle$s10)` on average, concentrating the burden on the livestock-dense South and Plains and widening the gap between where forage can be grown and where feed is needed.

These patterns suggest that warming will not simply lower alfalfa yields but redistribute them away from the regions that most depend on domestic forage. Because the steepest declines coincide with the country's most livestock-dense areas, and because those same regions already expose animals to direct heat stress, the pressure on feed supply and the pressure on herds are likely to compound rather than offset one another. Producers facing local forage shortfalls may need to absorb higher costs to ship feed in or to transition toward warm-season forages better suited to hotter conditions, adjustments that carry their own agronomic and nutritional trade-offs. These implications point to a widening mismatch between forage supply and feed demand that merits attention in regional adaptation planning, a theme we develop in the discussion.

This study makes three contributions. Methodologically, it is the first to apply the endogenous piecewise degree-day framework, previously reserved for grain crops [@tack2015; @schlenker2009], to U.S. alfalfa hay, and it does so over the most comprehensive record assembled for the crop, more than 70 years of county-level data covering `r fmt_num(objs$summary$n_counties, 0)` counties. Empirically, it delivers county-level yield-shock simulations across six warming scenarios, providing the regional and state-level detail needed to inform local supply-chain planning, and it links forage outcomes directly to the livestock sector, where county cattle inventory and alfalfa production are correlated at `r fmt_num(objs$assoc$cor_production, 2)`. Finally, by comparing alfalfa with non-alfalfa hay, it offers an empirical signal of how climate sensitivity differs across forage types, a distinction the existing literature has not drawn. In doing so, the study addresses the county-level gap in heat-threshold and yield-penalty estimates for U.S. alfalfa that prior work, focused on grains or on other regions [@gauly2013], has left open.

The remainder of the paper proceeds as follows. The next section describes the data sources, including the yield and weather records, and explains how we define the alfalfa growing season and construct the degree-day variables. The methods section then develops the model relating heat exposure to yield and the procedure for simulating future warming scenarios. The results section presents the nonlinear temperature-yield relationship and the geographically differentiated impacts of uniform warming, after which the discussion situates these findings within the broader context of climate adaptation, livestock production shifts, and potential crop switching under the Ricardian framework. A final section concludes.
